original: Planets Venus and Jupiter in conjunction - possible astronomical explanation for the Star of Bethlehem
Battle of Anchialus: Bulgarian army counterattacks the Byzantines
Hungary is established as a Christian kingdom by Stephen I of Hungary, celebrated today as State Foundation Day
Konrad von Wallenrode becomes the 24th Grand Master of the Teutonic Knights
Turkish admiral Chaireddin "Barbarossa" occupies Tunis
Iconoclasm reaches Antwerp, Belgium; the cathedral's interior is torn apart by Protestants
The Massacre of the Innocents: Prominent citizens in Timbuktu are rounded up, and some are killed by invading Moroccan forces, who then pillage the city, ending its golden age
First Dutch East India Company ships return from the Far East
Spanish garrison of Sluis surrenders to Dutch Stadtholder Maurice of Nassau
Nine of the ten "Pendle witches" are found guilty at trial of charges including murder, witchcraft, and talking to dogs and are hanged at Gallows Hill in Lancaster, England
England and Scotland sign the Treaty of Pacification
Battle of Lens: French Duc d'Enghien defeats the Spanish
Alaska is first sighted by a Russian expedition led by Danish explorer Vitus Bering
Hindi reform movement Brahmo Samaj is founded by Ram Mohan Roy and Dwarkanath Tagore in Calcutta
Wilberforce University is formed in Ohio
Rudolf Virchow's groundbreaking medical text "Cellular Pathology" is published in Berlin, establishing the basis for modern medical science
Skirmish in Jonesburg, Missouri
Eighth and last day of the battle at Deep Bottom Run, Virginia, with approximately 3,900 casualties
Government Kappeijne of Coppello resigns
In an epic first-round US men's singles tennis match, Palmer Presbrey takes 80 games to defeat T.S. Tailer, 19-21, 8-6, 6-1, 3-6, 6-4
The Transvaal National Union, a political organisation, is set up with J. Tudhope as president
Shechita (ritual slaughtering) prohibited in Switzerland
The dial telephone is patented
Great Britain beats France by 158 runs in Paris in cricket's only appearance at an Olympic Games
Japan's primary school law is amended to provide four years of mandatory schooling
The Fawcett Commission visits Mafeking concentration camp in Cape Colony
Congo Free State becomes Belgian Congo
US-supported opposition brings down Madriz in Nicaragua
Plant Quarantine Act goes into effect
First pilot to parachute from an aircraft (Adolphe Pégoud, France)
Battle of Gumbinnen, East Prussia: Russians defeat Germans
Battle of Morhange: German troops pursue French forces, resulting in thousands of casualties
Bavarian troops kill 50 inhabitants of Nomeny, France
German General von Bülow executes 211 Belgians
The German army captures Brussels as the Belgian army retreats to Antwerp
Chicago White Sox acquire "Shoeless" Joe Jackson from Cleveland for Robert Roth, Larry Chappell, Ed Klepfer, and $31,500; Jackson becomes involved in the "Black Sox Scandal" in 1919
WWI: Britain opens an offensive on the Western front
Wichita outfielder Joe Wilhoit (Western League) fails to get a hit, ending a 69-game streak (155 hits in 299 at bats for .505 average)
American Allen Woodring wins the 200 m wearing borrowed shoes at the Olympic Games in Antwerp, Belgium
First US radio station, 8MK (WWJ) in Detroit, begins daily broadcasting
Israel publishes its first medical journal "Ha-Refuah"
First Women's World Games (first track and field competition for women) are conducted over one day at Pershing Stadium in Paris
London dock strike ends
WJR-AM in Detroit, Michigan, begins radio transmissions
Japan establishes the public broadcasting company Nippon Hōsō Kyōkai (NHK)
First airship flight around Earth flying eastward is completed
DuMont's first TV broadcast for home reception in New York City
Military coup by General Pons and President Velasco Ibarra in Ecuador
First Black bowling league forms (National Bowling Association)
Russian offensive under General Zhukov against Japanese invasion in Mongolia
Police raid 11th district of Paris, taking 4,000+ Jewish males
Dim-out regulations implemented in San Francisco
Soviet offensive at Jassy and Kishinev
Seventeen-year-old Dodgers utility player Tommy Brown becomes the youngest player to hit an MLB home run in Brooklyn's 11-1 rout of the Pittsburgh Pirates at Ebbets Field
Soviet troops occupy Harbin and Mukden
Boston Braves hit a million in attendance for the first time
Turner Caldwell in D-558-I sets aircraft speed record at 1,131 kph
15th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Chi Cards 28, All-Stars 0 (101,220 attendees)
US expels Soviet Consul General in New York, Jacob Lomakin
78,382 fans watch the White Sox play the Indians in Cleveland
Hungary (Magyar People's Republic) adopts constitution
Las Lajas Sanctuary, a basilica built inside the Guáitara River Canyon in Colombia, is completed after 33 years
Russia publicly acknowledges hydrogen bomb test detonation
Archbishop Makarios of Cyprus petitions the UN for the Cypriot right to self-determination
Hundreds are killed in anti-French rioting in Morocco and Algeria
Republicans convene at Cow Palace
Chicago White Sox Bob Keegan no-hits Washington Senators, 6-0
USAF balloon, manned by David Simons, breaks an altitude record at 102,000 ft (31,089.6 m) over Minnesota and South Dakota
Chicago Cubs use first baseman Dale Long as their first major league lefty catcher since 1906
Belgium shortens military conscription to 12 months
Senegal breaks from the Mali Federation and declares independence

Soviet satellite Korabl-Sputnik 2 (Sputnik 5) returns to Earth with its passengers: two dogs, Belka and Strelka, two mice, two rats, flies, several plants, and fungi, the first Earth-born creatures to go into orbit and return alive
East Germany begins erecting a 5-foot-high wall (1.5 m) along the border with the West to replace the barbed wire put up on August 13
Philadelphia Phillies beat Milwaukee Braves 7-4 at County Stadium, ending a losing streak with the dubious MLB record of 23 straight losses
USSR performs a nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya, Soviet Union
Rolling Stones release their single "(I Can't Get No) Satisfaction" (their first #1 US hit) in the UK
Alvin Dark is fired, rehired, and fired again as manager of the MLB Kansas City A's by owner Charlie Finley
San Diego Stadium opens with a football game attended by a capacity crowd of 50,000 in San Diego, California; it is later renamed in honor of local sportswriter Jack Murphy
The USSR performs a nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeastern Kazakhstan
69 cm of rainfall in Nelson County, Virginia (state record)
Hurricane Dorothy kills 42 in Martinique
Ursula K. Le Guin is the first woman to win the Hugo Award for Best Novel with her work "The Left Hand of Darkness"
FBI begins covert investigation of journalist Daniel Schorr
The USSR performs an underground nuclear test
Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed is elected as the fifth President of India
Il-62 crashes south of Damascus, Syria, killing 126 people
Viking 1 is launched to orbit around Mars and performs a soft landing
Gunmen open fire on an Israeli El Al Airlines bus in London
Mark Vinchesi of Amherst, Massachusetts, keeps a frisbee aloft for 15.2 seconds
American singer Vikki Carr (39) weds businessman Michael Nilsson
India's Premier Charan Singh resigns
The East Coast Main Line rail route between England and Scotland is restored with the opening of the Penmanshiel Diversion
Cleveland Indians pitcher Dan Spillner, with a 5.45 ERA, is two outs from a no-hitter when Chicago White Sox rookie Leo Sutherland hits a single
NY Yankee Bob Watson hits Seattle Kingdome speaker, second consecutive day
Pittsburgh Pirate Omar Moreno steals a record 70 bases for the third consecutive season
Reinhold Messner of Italy is the first to complete a solo ascent of Mt Everest
UN Security Council condemns (14-0, US abstains) Israel's declaration that all of Jerusalem is its capital
Don Lever becomes the first captain of the NHL's New Jersey Devils
US Marines land in Beirut, Lebanon
The South African anti-apartheid umbrella organization, United Democratic Front (UDF), is launched at Rocklands Community Centre in Cape Town, South Africa
Hanspeter Beck of South Australia finishes a 3,875-mile, 51-day trip from Western Australia to Melbourne on a unicycle
Israel ships 96 TOW missiles to Iran on behalf of the US
Libya expels thousands of Tunisian and Egyptian gas workers
Mets'Dwight Goodenstrikes out 16 on his way to his 13th consecutive win
MLB Philadelphia Phillies' Don Carman's perfect game bid is broken in the 9th when he surrenders a leadoff double in a 1-0 win over the Giants at Candlestick Park in San Francisco
Postal worker Patrick Sherrill shoots 14 fellow workers dead in Edmond, Oklahoma
"Black Saturday" of the Yellowstone fire in Yellowstone National Park, Wyoming
6.5 earthquake strikes India and Nepal, killing thousands
Peru becomes a member of the Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works
Yordanka Donkova of Bulgaria sets the women's world 100 m hurdles record (12.21) in Stara Zagora, Bulgaria
American Janet Evans swims the women's 800 m freestyle world record of 8:16.22 at the Pan Pacific Championships in Tokyo, Japan
Dredger "Bow Belle" collides with pleasure boat "Marchioness" on the River Thames in central London; "Marchioness" sinks in 30 seconds, drowning 51
Said Aouita runs the 3000 m in a world record time of 7:29.45
Gene Michaelbecomes the New York Yankees' general manager, replacing Harding Peterson
Iraq moves Western hostages to military installations as human shields
New York Yankee Kevin Maas is the quickest to reach 15 home runs in approximately 132 at-bats
"Sailor Moon" by Naoko Takeuchi, one of the most popular manga of all time, is first published in Japan
Estonia formally declares its independence from the USSR
Norbert Rózsa swims a world record in the 100 m breaststroke (1:01.29)
The United Democratic Front, one of the most prominent anti-apartheid movements, comprising over 400 worker, church, civic, and student organizations, dissolves
England scores a world ODI record of 363 runs in 55 overs against Pakistan
Colin Jackson runs a world record 110 m hurdles in 12.91 seconds
Howard Stern is fired from WLUP-AM in Chicago, Illinois
Oslo Peace Accords are signed between Israel and the Palestine Liberation Organization after secret negotiations in Norway, a public ceremony in Washington, D.C. is held the following month
109.8°F (43.2°C) is recorded in Cordoba, Spain
Catholic Archbishop Antonio Quarracino states that all homosexuals should leave Argentina
Ferry boat sinks in Chandpur, Bangladesh, killing 300-350
Indians' José Mesa sets a record with his 37th consecutive save
India defeats Pakistan in the Under-15 World Challenge Final at Lord's
Souhane massacre in Algeria: Over 64 villagers are killed, and 15 women are kidnapped by Islamist groups
Supreme Court of Canada rules that Quebec cannot legally secede from Canada without federal government approval

US embassy bombings: US military launches cruise missile attacks against alleged al-Qaida camps in Afghanistan and a suspected chemical plant in Sudan in retaliation for the August 7 bombings of American embassies in Kenya and Tanzania
A group of Iraqis opposed to the regime of Saddam Hussein takes over the Iraqi Embassy in Berlin for five hours before releasing their hostages and surrendering
Russian long-distance swimmer Larisa Ilchenko wins inaugural women's open-water marathon (10 km) in 1:59:27.7 at the Beijing Olympics
Spanair Flight 5022 from Madrid to Gran Canaria skids off the runway and crashes at Barajas Airport, killing 146 people in the crash and 8 more afterward. Only 18 people survive
20 people are killed in a riot in Caracas, Venezuela
South Africa becomes the top-ranked Test cricket nation after defeating England
Nine Islamist militants are killed by Russian police in North Caucasus
Thirty students at West Point Military Academy are injured in a mass pillow fight
Canoeists Max Rendschmidt and Marcus Gross are part of the winning German crew in the K-4 1000 m at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, wrapping up the K-2 / K-4 1000 m double
Chinese diver Chen Aisen achieves the Olympic 10 m platform double in Rio de Janeiro; having already won the synchronized event, he wins the individual gold by a comfortable margin
Controversial South African middle distance runner Caster Semenya retains her Olympic women's 800 m title in a time of 1:55.28 in the final in Rio de Janeiro

Four women's high jump competitors tie at clearing the winning height of 1.97 m at the Rio Olympics; all then fail at 2.00 m; Ruth Beitia of Spain wins the gold based on having no misses prior to the bar being raised to 2.00 m
German canoeist Sebastian Brendel teams with Jan Vandrey to win the C-2 1000m at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, wrapping up the C-1 / C-2 1000 m double

In a blanket finish, American triathlete Gwen Jorgensen takes the Olympic gold medal at the Rio de Janeiro Games in 1:56:16, 0.40 seconds ahead of Nicola Spirig Hug of Switzerland and Great Britain's Vicky Holland by 0:45
In team sports at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, China beats Serbia 3-1 for the women's indoor volleyball gold; in the men's water polo final, Serbia downs Croatia 11-7 for its first Olympic title
N. K. Jemisin is the first African American author to win the Hugo Award for Best Novel with her work "The Fifth Season"
Suicide bombing in Turkish city Gaziantep during a wedding party kills at least 51 people
The Hungarian crew wins the canoe sprint K-4 500 m at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics; Danuta Kozák becomes the only female to win K-1, K-2, and K-4 at the same Olympics
Colorado man Christopher Lee Watts is charged with the murder of his wife and two daughters
Measles cases reach a record high in Europe, with 41,000 infected in the first six months of 2018 and 37 deaths, according to the WHO
Polish immigrant and former volunteer Nazi guard Jakiw Palij (95) is deported from the US in New York to Germany
Venezuela issues a new currency, the "Bolivar Soberano," in an attempt to stop runaway hyperinflation
Computer systems in 22 small Texas towns are hacked and held for ransom in a coordinated attack, prompting an FBI investigation
NASA confirms a mission to Jupiter's ice-covered moon Europa to search for alien life, launching in 2025
22 million people are now at risk of starvation in the Horn of Africa, an increase of 9 million since January, according to the UN, following the worst drought in 40 years
Misuzulu ka Zwelithini is crowned Zulu King at KwaKhangelamankengane Palace in KwaZulu-Natal province, South Africa
Premiere of saxophone concerto "Adagio (For Wadada Leo Smith)" by Tyshawn Sorey at the Lucerne Festival; wins 2024 Pulitzer Prize for Music
Ecuador votes in a referendum against drilling for oil in Yasuní National Park, a world biosphere reserve inhabited by Indigenous groups in the Amazon
FIFA Women's World Cup Final, Stadium Australia, Sydney: Spanish captain Olga Carmona scores the only goal of the game as La Furia Roja score a 1-0 win over England
Guatemala's presidential election is won by anti-corruption candidate Bernardo Arévalo, who defeats former first lady Sandra Torres
Russia's Luna 25 spacecraft crashes into the Moon, according to Roscosmos, the country's space agency
Tropical Storm Hilary makes landfall on Mexico's Baja Peninsula, the first tropical storm to hit the region in 84 years
2,000 Muslim prisoners killed after the Siege of Acre, reportedly on the orders of Crusader KingRichard I, during the Third Crusade
Francis Drakerenames his flagship the "Pelican" to the "Golden Hind" in honor of his patron, Sir Christopher Hatton
Former Grand Pensionary of HollandJohan de Wittand his brother Cornelis are brutally murdered, and their livers are supposedly cooked and eaten by an angry mob in The Hague
George Washingtonbegins moving his troops south to fight Cornwallis
Battle of Fallen Timbers: Major General "Mad Anthony" Wayne defeats a joint British-Native American force at Fallen Timbers, Ohio in the final battle of the Northwest Indian War
Joseph Haydnreturns to Vienna from England
Gioachino Rossini's opera "Le Comte Ory" premieres in Paris
PresidentAndrew Johnsonissues Proclamation 157, declaring the "insurrection" in Texas over and officially ending the US Civil War
Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky's "1812 Overture" debuts in Moscow
Second Salon exhibition by the Society of Independent Artists is held in Paris and includesGeorges Seurat's "A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte"
Chinese revolutionarySun Yat-senforms the first chapter of Tongmenghui, a union of secret societies determined to bring down the Qing dynasty

Washington Senators' future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherWalter Johnsonwins an AL-record 15th consecutive game, beating the Cleveland Naps 4-2; in the nightcap, Carl Cashion no-hits the Naps 2-0 in 6 innings
American Professional Football Association forms withJim Thorpeas president and later becomes the National Football League (NFL)
NY Yankees future Baseball Hall of Fame first basemanLou Gehrighits his record 23rd and final grand slam in an 11-3 win over the Philadelphia A's at Shibe Park
British Prime Minister Churchill says of the Royal Air Force, "Never in the field of human conflict was so much owed by so many to so few"
Louis Buchalteris indicted on murder charges in Los Angeles for the killing of Harry Greenberg, a mob associate of casino ownerMeyer Lanskyand mobsterBugsy Siegel
French GeneralCharles de Gaullereturns to France
12th Venice Film Festival: "Rashomon" directed byAkira Kurosawawins the Golden Lion
Soviet leaderJoseph Stalinmeets Chinese PremierZhou Enlai
General Fazlollah Zahedi arrests Iranian Prime Minister MohammadMossadeghin a CIA-supported coup d'état
New York Yankees player Phil Linz plays harmonica on bus despite managerYogi Berra's orders
US PresidentLyndon B. Johnsonsigns the Economic Opportunity Act, an anti-poverty measure totaling nearly $1 billion
Eddie Mathews andHank Aaron(1954-65) surpass Babe Ruth and Lou Gehrig by hitting 772 home runs while playing together on the same team
Nelson Rockefelleris selected as US Vice President by PresidentGerald Ford
Nolan Ryan's pitch is measured at a record 161.6 kph (100.4 mph)
First NL pitcher to strike out 200+ in first two seasons (Dwight Gooden)
MLB New York Mets infielderHoward Johnsonjoins Barry Bonds andWillie Maysby hitting 30 home runs and stealing 30 bases in a season
George Steinbrennersteps down as New York Yankee owner
Dolphins'Dan MarinosurpassesJoe Montanaas the highest-paid NFL player with a 5-year extension for $25 million
Mother Teresais hospitalized with malaria

PGA Championship Men's Golf, Valhalla GC:Tiger Woodswins back-to-back PGA titles after a three-hole playoff with Bob May, becoming the first to win three majors in a calendar year sinceBen Hoganin 1953
American swimmerMichael Phelpswraps up the 100/200 m butterfly double at the Athens Olympics when he wins the 100 m (51.25 OR) ahead of teammate Ian Crocker
Ethiopian runnersKenenisa Bekele(27:05.10 OR) and Sileshi Sihine go 1-2 in the 10,000 m at the Athens Olympics
Usain Boltof Jamaica wraps up the 100/200 m double in style with a new 200 m world record of 19.30 seconds at the Beijing Olympics
"One Direction: This Is Us," a 3D concert film ofOne Directiondirected by Morgan Spurlock, premieres in London

South Korean golferInbee Parkwins the inaugural Olympic women's individual gold with a final score of 16 under par in Rio de Janeiro;Lydia Ko(New Zealand) 11 under par; Shanshan Feng (China) 10 under par
Star British runnerMo Farahwins the men's 5000 m in 13:03.30 at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics and wraps up the 5k/10k double, which he also completed in London 2012
Pope Francisreleases a letter to all Catholics condemning sexual abuse atrocities and clerical cover-ups, stating, "We showed no care for the little ones; we abandoned them"
Italian Prime MinisterGiuseppe Conteresigns afterMatteo Salvinicalls for a vote of no confidence
Former adviser toDonald Trump, Steve Bannon, is arrested and charged with fraud over a fundraising campaign to build a wall on the Mexican border
Kamala Harrisaccepts her nomination for vice president, becoming the first US woman of color on a major-party ticket, saying "there is no vaccine for racism"
Russian opposition figureAlexei Navalnyfalls into a coma after a suspected poisoning (later confirmed to be Novichok poisoning)

Battle of Thermopylae: In one of history's most famous last stands, 7,000 warriors from an alliance of Greek states led by Leonidas I block the pass of Thermopylae for a week against an invading Persian army under Xerxes I, estimated at 120,000 to 300,000 strong [date is approximate]
America's Great White Fleet arrives in Sydney, Australia, greeted with a tremendous welcome; 221 American sailors desert to stay in Australia
First Polish fighter squadrons fight alongside Allies in the Battle of Britain
250,000 Soviet and Warsaw Pact troops invade Czechoslovakia at night to suppress the Prague Spring
NASA launches Voyager 2 towards Jupiter and Saturn, later reaching Uranus and Neptune
Shahrukh Mirza, Ruler of Persia and Transoxonia (1405-47), born in Samarkand, Uzbekistan (d. 1447)
Antoine Perrenot de Granvelle, Spanish cardinal and viceroy of Naples (1571-75), born in Besançon, France (d. 1586)
Petrus Opmeer, Dutch church historian and humanist (Historia Martyrum), born in Amstersdam (d. 1595)
Jacopo Peri, Italian composer and singer who wrote the 1st recognized opera "Dafne", born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1633)
Thomas Corneille, French playwright (Timocrates), born in Rouen, France (d. 1709)
Louis Bourdaloue, French Jesuit preacher, born in Bourges, France (d. 1704)
Cornelis Van Aerssen, Dutch governor of Suriname (1638-88), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1688)
Henry Every, English pirate, captained the most profitable raid in history on a Mughal pilgrim ship, born in Newton Ferrers, England (date of death unknown)
Bernardus Smijtegelt, Dutch vicar, and writer (“Het Gekrookte Riet” (The Bruised Reed)), born in Goes, Netherlands (d. 1739)

Thomas Simpson, English mathematician (rule of Simpson), born in Sutton Cheney, Leicestershire (d. 1761)

Bernardo Bittoni, Italian composer, born in Fabriano, Papal States (d. 1829)
Charles Ferdinand Bentinck, Dutch military officer (Governor of Suriname 1809-11), born in The Hague, Dutch Republic (d. 1811)
Bernardo O'Higgins, Chilean independence leader who freed Chile from Spanish rule (Supreme Director of Chile 1817-23), born in Chillán, Captaincy General of Chile (d. 1842)
Jöns Jakob Berzelius, Swedish chemist, born in Linköping, Sweden (d. 1848)
Oliver Hazard Perry, American Naval hero (Battle of Lake Erie), born in South Kingstown, Rhode Island (d. 1819)
José Bernardo Alcedo, Peruvian composer, born in Lima, Peru (d. 1878)
Heinrich von Gagern, German liberal politician, born in Bayreuth, Bavaria, Germany (d. 1880)
Cadwalader Ringgold, American Commander (Union Navy), born in Washington County, Maryland (d. 1867)
Gilman Marston, American Brigadier General (Union Army) and politician (R-NH), born in Orford, New Hampshire (d. 1890)
Adolphe Fétis, Belgian-French pianist and composer, born in Paris (d. 1873)
Absalom Baird, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Rockland, Maine (d. 1905)
Josef Strauss, Austrian composer (Dorfschwalben aus Austria), born in Mariahilf, Austria (d. 1870)
Thaddeus S. C. Lowe, American aeronaut and inventor, born in Jefferson Mills, Coos County, New Hampshire (d. 1913)
Juliaan de Vriendt, Flemish painter, born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1935)
Albert Chmielowski, Polish Catholic Saint, born in Igołomia, Małopolskie, Congress Poland (d. 1916)
Andrew Greenwood, English cricket batsman (2 Tests, HS 49; Yorkshire CCC), born in Huddersfield, England (d. 1889)
Bolesław Prus, Polish writer (The Outpost), born in Hrubieszów, Congress Poland (d. 1912)
Innokenti F Annenski, Russian poet and interpreter (Laodania), born in Omsk, Russian Empire (d. 1909) [NS Sept 1]

Bernard Tancred, South African cricket batsman (2 Tests; Northern Cape), born in Port Elizabeth, Cape Province (d. 1911)
Ellen Roosevelt, American tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1890), born in Rosedale, New York (d. 1954)

Eliel Saarinen, Finnish-American architect (Helsinki Central Station; Cranbrook Educational Community (Bloomfield Hills, Michigan), designer, and educator, born in Rantasalmi, Grand Duchy of Finland (d. 1950) (d. 1950)
William Henry Bell, English composer, born in St Albans, England (d. 1946)
Edgar Albert Guest, English-American poet and newspaperman, born in Birmingham, England (d. 1959)
Dino Campana, Italian poet (Canti orfici), born in Marradi, Italy (d. 1932)
Paul Tillich, German-American theologist (Die sozial Entscheidung), born in Starzeddel, Brandenburg, Germany (d. 1965)
George Sklavos, Greek composer, born in Brailov, Romania (d. 1976)
Witold Friemann, Polish composer, pianist, conductor and pedagogue, born in Konin, Poland (d. 1977)
H. P. Lovecraft, American horror writer (At the Mountains of Madness, Weird Tales), born in Providence, Rhode Island (d. 1937)
Tarjei Vesaas, Norwegian poet and author (Det Store Spelet), born in Vinje, Telemark, Norway (d. 1970)
Gerard Kraus, Dutch psychiatrist and director Santpoort (Insanity in the Netherlands), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1956)

Leopold Infeld, Polish nuclear physicist, writer (The Evolution of Physics: The Growth of Ideas from Early Concepts to Relativity and Quanta (with Albert Einstein); Whom the Gods Love: The Story of Évariste Galois), and peace activist, born in Kraków, Congress Poland (d. 1968)
Vilhelm Moberg, Swedish author (The Emigrants) and historian, born in Moshultamåla, Sweden (d. 1973)
Hanns Lilje, German Lutheran bishop and antifascist (Church und Welt), born in Hanover, Germany (d. 1977)
Murt [Edward James] O'Donoghue, New Zealand snooker player (1st witnessed 147 break), born in Te Aroha, New Zealand (d. 1994)
Ernest Marke, Sierra Leonean seaman and club owner, born in Sierra Leone (d. 1995)
(Weldon Leo) "Jack" Teagarden, American jazz trombonist (Paul Whiteman, 1933-38; Louis Armstrong's All-Stars, 1946-51), singer ("Basin Street Blues"), and bandleader, born in Vernon, Texas (d. 1964)
Duncan Macrae, Scottish actor (Casino Royale, The Little Kidnappers), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1967)
Jean Gebser [Hans Gebser], German-Swiss philosopher, author (Ursprung und Gegenwart (The Ever-Present Origin)), linguist, and poet, born in Posen, German Empire (d. 1973)
Henry Austin, British tennis player (3-time Grand Slam runner-up), born in London, England (d. 2000)
Alan Reed, American actor (Breakfast at Tiffany's) and voice artist (Fred Flintstone, Lady and the Tramp), born in New York City (d. 1977)
Anatole Fistoulari, Ukrainian conductor, born in Kyiv, Ukraine (d. 1995)
Stanley Devon, British photographer (British press photographer of the year 1948, 50), born in London (d. 1995)
Jeanne Stern, German screenwriter (The Condemned Village), born in Bedous, Aquitaine (d. 1998)
John Reginald Bevins, British Conservative politician, born in Liverpool, Lancashire (d. 1996)
Kingsley Davis, American sociologist and demographer (coined the term "population explosion"), born in Tuxedo, Texas (d. 1997)
Alby Roberts, New Zealand cricket fast bowler (5 Tests, 7 wickets; Canterbury, Otago), born in Christchurch, New Zealand (d. 1978)
André Morell [Cecil André Mesritz], British actor (Ben-Hur, The Message, Hound of the Baskervilles), born in London, England (d. 1978)

Eero Saarinen, Finnish-American architect (Dulles Airport (Virginia); Gateway Arch (St. Louis, Missouri); IBM Building (Rochester, Minnesota); MIT Chapel (Cambridge, Massachusetts)), born in Kirkkonummi, Finland (d. 1961)[1]
Philip Kapleau, American Zen teacher and founder (Rochester Zen Center in New Haven), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 2004)
Paul Felix Schmidt, Estonian-German chess player, born in Narva, Estonia (d. 1984)
Gloria Parker [Rosenthal], American marimbist, glasspiel player, songwriter ("Clap Your Hands and Shake Your Blues Away"), and bandleader, born in Brooklyn, NYC (d. 2022)[1]
Jack Wilson, Australian cricket spin bowler (1 Test; 43 wickets on England Tour 1956), born in Albert Park, Victoria (d. 1985)
Tetsuzo Akutsu, Japanese surgeon who built the first artificial heart, born in Japan (d. 2007)
Jim Reeves, American country singer ("He'll Have To Go"; "Distant Drums"), and actor (Kimberley Jim), born in Panola County, Texas (d. 1964)
Frank Rosolino, American jazz trombonist (The Frank Rosolino Sextet), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1978)
Nobby Wirkowski, American-Canadian football player and coach (Toronto Argonauts), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2014)
Geriatric1927 [Peter Oakley], English video blogger, born in Norwich, Norfolk, England (d. 2014)
Yootha Joyce, British actress (Man About the House, George and Mildred), born in Wandsworth, London (d. 1980)
Ed Sandford, Canadian ice hockey forward (NHL All-Star 1951, 52, 53, 54, 55; Boston Bruins, Detroit Red Wings), born in New Toronto, Ontario (d. 2023)
Piet Kraak, Dutch soccer goalkeeper (33 caps; Stormvogels, Elinkwijk), born in Alblasserdam, Netherlands (d. 1984)
Mario Bernardi, Canadian pianist and conductor, born in Kirkland Lake, Ontario (d. 2013)
Bernhard "Bernd" Becher, German artist and photographer (industrialist builder 19th/20th), born in Siegen, Germany (d. 2007)
Don King, American boxing promoter best known for his association with Mike Tyson and for his unusual hairstyles, born in Cleveland, Ohio
Anthony Ainley, British actor (Doctor Who), born in Stanmore, Middlesex (d. 2004)
Atholl Mckinnon, South African cricket spin bowler (8 Tests, 26 wickets; Eastern Province, Transvaal), born in Port Elizabeth, Cape Province, South Africa (d. 1983)
Vasily Aksyonov, Soviet-Russian novelist (The Burn), born in Kazan, Soviet Union (d. 2009)
Don Fuqua, American Democratic politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Florida), born in Jacksonville, Florida
George J. Mitchell, American lawyer and politician (Senate Majority Leader 1989-95), born in Waterville, Maine
"Sneaky" Pete Kleinow, American country-rock musician (Flying Burrito Brothers), born in South Bend, Indiana (d. 2007)
